docs.title: Welcome to RemoteX
docs.text: RemoteX is a visual command launcher for Linux. Build a grid of clickable buttons — each one runs a shell command, locally or on a remote server over SSH.

docs.title: Who is RemoteX for?
docs.text:
If you're new to Linux, RemoteX saves you from memorizing commands. Click a button to check your disk usage, update your system, or reboot — no terminal needed. RemoteX comes with 34 ready-to-use buttons out of the box.

If you manage servers, RemoteX is a visual SSH command launcher. Assign buttons to one or more remote machines, run commands across your infrastructure with a single click, and see the output instantly.

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
1. Install RemoteX
  2. Launch the app — 34 default buttons are ready to use
  3. Follow the Quick Start guide to create your first custom button

!!! tip "Try Pro free for 14 days" The Pro build includes an automatic 14-day trial — every feature unlocked, no card, no email. Just download the Pro build and launch it.

docs.title: What's in the box
docs.text: Feature Available in 34 ready-to-use buttons (Linux Essentials + Development) Free Unlimited local command execution Free Custom buttons Free (up to 3) · Pro (unlimited) Categories, icons, colors, tooltips Free SSH machines Pro Multi-machine buttons Pro Multi-select + group actions Pro Button themes Pro Config backup / restore Pro Execution profiles (run-as-user + sudo password) Pro MCP server (AI assistant integration) Pro

docs.title: Per-button colors
docs.text:
Each button has two independent color settings, accessible in the button editor:

  • Color — background color of the tile
  • Text color — color of the label

Both use a 40-color GNOME palette with a hex input field for custom values.

docs.title: Button size
docs.text:
Preferences → Button Grid Layout → Button size

Size Dimensions Small 80 × 80 px Medium 120 × 120 px Large 160 × 160 px

docs.title: Show icon only / label only
docs.text:
In the button editor:

  • Hide label — displays the icon only (useful for small buttons or well-known icons)
  • Hide icon — displays the label only

docs.title: Button themes (Pro)
docs.text:
!!! tip "Pro feature" Button themes require RemoteX Pro.

Preferences → Button Appearance → Button theme

Theme Style Bold Solid colored tiles with strong contrast (default) Phone keys Compact flat tiles, reminiscent of a dial pad Neon Dark background with glowing accent borders Retro Terminal-inspired monochrome with scanlines

docs.title: Custom CSS (Pro)
docs.text:
Write your own .css file targeting .button-tile for full control:

.button-tile {
  background: linear-gradient(135deg, #6a0dad, #9b30d9);
  border-radius: 12px;
  border: 2px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.2);
}

.button-tile:hover {
  box-shadow: 0 6px 18px rgba(0,0,0,0.4);
}

.button-tile .tile-label {
  color: #ffffff;
  font-weight: bold;
}

Load it via Preferences → Button Appearance → Custom CSS file → Browse. Use the Export template button to get a starter file with all available selectors and named colors.

docs.title: Color scheme
docs.text:
Preferences → Interface → Color scheme

  • Follow system — respects your desktop dark/light setting
  • Light — always light
  • Dark — always dark

docs.title: Default buttons
docs.text: RemoteX ships with 34 ready-to-use buttons seeded on first launch. They are visible, executable and deletable on the free tier.

docs.title: Linux Essentials (20 buttons)
docs.text: Button Command Disk Usage df -h Memory Usage free -h CPU Load uptime + top snapshot Temperature sensors (requires lm-sensors) Running Processes ps aux sorted by CPU Network Interfaces ip addr Active Connections ss -tp state established Open Ports ss -tulpn Block Devices lsblk Largest Directories du -sh /* System Info uname -a + lsb_release Logged-in Users w Last Logins last Failed Services systemctl --failed System Journal journalctl -n 50 Kernel Messages dmesg Clear Trash Empties ~/.local/share/Trash System Update apt / dnf / pacman auto-detected Reboot systemctl reboot Shutdown systemctl poweroff

docs.title: Development (14 buttons)
docs.text: Git Status · Git Log · Git Diff · Docker PS · Docker PS All · Docker Images · Docker Clean · Tail Syslog · Disk I/O · Python Version · Pip Outdated · Node Version · NPM Outdated · Listening Services

docs.title: Custom buttons
docs.text:
Click + (or Ctrl+N) to create a custom button. See Your first button for a full walkthrough.

!!! info "Free tier limit" The free tier allows 3 custom buttons. Default buttons are not counted toward this limit. Upgrade to Pro for unlimited custom buttons.

docs.title: Grid layout
docs.text:
Adjust the number of columns and button size in Preferences → Button Grid Layout:

  • Buttons per row — 1 to 20 columns (applied live)
  • Button size — Small (80×80) · Medium (120×120) · Large (160×160)

docs.title: Search
docs.text: Press the search icon in the header bar (or start typing anywhere) to filter buttons by name in real time.

docs.title: Keyboard shortcuts
docs.text: Shortcut Action Ctrl+N Add a new button Ctrl+, Open Preferences Ctrl+? Show keyboard shortcuts Ctrl+Q Quit

docs.title: Categories
docs.text: Categories group your buttons into tabs displayed just below the header bar.

docs.title: Assigning a category
docs.text:
When creating or editing a button, type a category name in the Category field. Buttons with the same category name are grouped together automatically.

You can also reassign a button quickly: right-click → Move to category.

docs.title: Filtering by category
docs.text: Click any category pill in the tab bar to show only the buttons in that category. Click All to return to the full grid.

docs.title: Hiding a category
docs.text:
Right-click a category pill → Hide category. The category and all its buttons disappear from the grid.

To show it again: Preferences → Categories → toggle the category back on.

!!! note Hiding a category does not delete any buttons — they are simply filtered out of view.

docs.title: Removing a category
docs.text: Categories are created and removed automatically. To remove a category, reassign or delete all buttons that belong to it — the tab disappears on its own.

docs.title: Creating a button
docs.text: Click + in the header bar (or press Ctrl+N) to open the button editor.

docs.title: Required fields
docs.text:
Name — The label shown on the button.

Command — The shell command to execute. Examples:

df -h                        # disk usage
ping -c 4 8.8.8.8            # network test
sudo systemctl restart nginx # restart a service

docs.title: Optional fields
docs.text: Field Description Category Groups buttons under a tab in the header bar Icon Choose from hundreds of built-in icons Color Background color of the button tile Text color Color of the button label Tooltip Custom text shown on hover (defaults to the command) Confirm before running Shows a confirmation dialog before executing Output mode Silent / Show output / Open in terminal Hide label Show icon only Hide icon Show label only

docs.title: Output modes
docs.text:
Silent — The command runs in the background. A small toast notification confirms success or failure.

Show output — A dialog opens with the full stdout / stderr after the command finishes. Useful for commands that return information (disk usage, git log…).

Open in terminal — The command runs in your system terminal emulator. Use this for interactive commands (top, htop, vim…).

!!! tip The Show output mode also opens automatically when a command fails, regardless of the mode selected.

docs.title: Editing and deleting
docs.text:
Right-click any button to open the context menu:

  • Edit — reopen the button editor
  • Duplicate — create a copy
  • Move to category — reassign quickly
  • Delete — permanently remove the button

docs.title: Reordering
docs.text: Drag and drop buttons to reorder them within the grid.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Linux (X11 or Wayland)
  • Python 3.10 or later
  • GTK 4 + libadwaita

docs.title: From source
docs.text: This is the recommended method until the Flatpak package is available.

docs.title: 1. Install system dependencies
docs.text:
=== "Debian / Ubuntu / Linux Mint"

```bash
sudo apt install python3-gi gir1.2-gtk-4.0 gir1.2-adw-1 \
  libgtk-4-1 python3-gi-cairo libglib2.0-bin wmctrl git
```

=== "Fedora"

```bash
sudo dnf install python3-gobject gtk4 libadwaita \
  python3-cairo glib2 wmctrl git
```

=== "Arch Linux"

```bash
sudo pacman -S python-gobject gtk4 libadwaita \
  python-cairo glib2 wmctrl git
```

docs.title: 2. Clone and run
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/neurocontrarian/remotex.git
cd remotex
./run_dev.sh

run_dev.sh handles everything automatically:

  • Creates a Python virtual environment
  • Installs Python dependencies (fabric, tomli_w, paramiko)
  • Compiles the GSettings schema
  • Launches RemoteX

The first launch seeds your button grid with 34 default buttons.

docs.title: Flatpak (coming soon)
docs.text: A Flatpak package will be published on Flathub. Until then, use the source install above.

docs.title: Updating
docs.text:
cd remotex
git pull
./run_dev.sh

docs.title: Multi-select
docs.text:
!!! tip "Pro feature" Multi-select requires RemoteX Pro.

Multi-select lets you perform bulk operations on several buttons at once.

docs.title: Entering select mode
docs.text:
Click the select icon (☑) in the header bar. The grid switches to selection mode — clicking a button toggles its selection instead of running the command.

Press Escape or click the select icon again to exit.

docs.title: Selecting buttons
docs.text:
Click any button to toggle its selection (highlighted in blue).

Rubber-band — click and drag on an empty area of the grid to draw a selection rectangle. All buttons the rectangle overlaps are added to the selection.

docs.title: Group actions
docs.text:
Once buttons are selected, an action bar appears at the bottom of the window:

Action Description Delete Permanently remove all selected buttons (confirmation required) Category Assign all selected buttons to a category (type a new name or pick an existing one) Machine Assign all selected buttons to an SSH machine

docs.title: RemoteX Pro
docs.text: RemoteX comes in two builds: Free (open source, local execution only) and Pro (proprietary, full feature set with SSH and AI integration).

docs.title: Free vs Pro
docs.text:
Free Pro Local command execution Unlimited Unlimited Custom buttons Unlimited Unlimited Default buttons Visible, executable, deletable Editable SSH machines — Unlimited Multi-machine buttons — ✓ Multi-select + group actions — ✓ Button themes (Bold, Phone, Neon, Retro…) — ✓ Custom CSS theme — ✓ Execution profiles — ✓ Config backup / restore — ✓ MCP server (AI integration) — ✓ AI button execution — ✓

!!! note Free and Pro are separate downloads, not the same binary with a license unlock. The Free build does not contain any Pro code.

docs.title: Pricing
docs.text:
$29 / year — yearly license, automatically renewed at $29 each year unless cancelled.

You can cancel anytime from the LemonSqueezy customer portal (the link is in your purchase email). Cancellation prevents the next renewal — your current period continues until its expiry date, and Pro features stay available until then.

A lifetime option may be offered later as a limited event. At launch, only the yearly license is available.

Get a license →

docs.title: 14-day free trial
docs.text:
The Pro build includes an automatic 14-day trial that starts the first time you launch it. No license key, no payment, no email capture — every Pro feature is available immediately.

A few days before the trial ends, RemoteX shows an in-app offer with a discount code. After day 14, Pro features become read-only (greyed out) — your buttons, machines and settings are never deleted.

To continue using Pro, activate a license from Preferences → License.

docs.title: Activating your license
docs.text:
1. Open Preferences (Ctrl+,)
  2. Scroll to the License section
  3. Enter the email address used for purchase
  4. Paste your license key
  5. Click Activate Pro

An internet connection is required for the initial activation. The license is then validated offline daily.

docs.title: Yearly renewal
docs.text:
A yearly license expires 365 days after activation. RemoteX shows a warning toast 30 days before expiry.

After expiry, free-tier limits apply again — your buttons and machines are not deleted, but Pro-only features (SSH, MCP, multi-select, themes, etc.) become unavailable until you renew.

Renew from Preferences → License → Renew license.

docs.title: Deactivating
docs.text:
To remove the license from a device: Preferences → License → Deactivate license.

This frees up an activation slot so you can use the same key on a different device. Your Pro license allows up to 3 simultaneous activations on devices you personally use — see License & Devices for details.

Free-tier limits apply immediately after deactivation. Your buttons are not deleted; Pro features are restored when you reactivate.

docs.title: Execution profiles (Pro)
docs.text:
Create reusable execution contexts that combine a target user, a working directory, and a sudo password into a single named profile.

Assign a profile to any button — when it runs, the command executes as the specified user in the specified directory, with the sudo password fed automatically (no terminal prompt).

Profiles work for both local and remote (SSH) execution, including Open in terminal mode.

Manage profiles from the hamburger menu → Manage Profiles.

docs.title: Backup and restore (Pro)
docs.text:
Export and import your full configuration from Preferences:

  • Buttons & Settings backup — exports buttons and preferences to a .rxbackup file
  • Machines backup — exports SSH machine definitions to a .rxmachines file (SSH private keys are never included)

Restore from the same Preferences section. Importing buttons merges with existing default buttons — newly added defaults are never lost.

docs.title: AI integration (Pro)
docs.text: The MCP server lets AI assistants like Claude, Cursor or Open WebUI read, edit and execute your buttons through a single secure connection. See AI Integration (MCP) for full setup and security details.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: Get RemoteX running and your first button working in about two minutes.

docs.title: 1. Launch RemoteX
docs.text:
After installing RemoteX, run it from the project folder:

./run_dev.sh

The first launch seeds your grid with 34 default buttons organised in two categories: Linux Essentials and Development. No setup needed — click any of them to run the command immediately.

docs.title: 2. Run a default button
docs.text:
Click Disk Usage to see your filesystem at a glance. A toast notification confirms success. Click it again while holding no modifier to just run it silently.

For commands that produce output (like Disk Usage), the result appears in a small dialog automatically.

docs.title: 3. Create your first custom button
docs.text:
1. Press Ctrl+N (or click + in the header bar)
  2. Fill in Label — the text shown on the button
  3. Fill in Command — any shell command, for example ping -c 4 8.8.8.8
  4. Click Save

Your button appears in the grid. Click it to run the command.

!!! tip The free tier allows 3 custom buttons. RemoteX Pro removes this limit.

docs.title: 4. Organise with categories
docs.text: In the button editor, type a name in the Category field (e.g. Networking). A pill tab appears in the category bar below the header. Click the pill to filter the grid to that category.

docs.title: 5. Next steps
docs.text: What you want to do Where to look Understand every button field Button Editor Explore every UI element Main Window Connect a remote server SSH Machines Adjust columns, size, theme Preferences See real-world examples Use Cases

docs.title: SSH Machines
docs.text:
!!! tip "Pro feature" SSH machines require RemoteX Pro.

RemoteX connects to remote servers using SSH key authentication. No passwords are ever stored.

docs.title: Adding a machine
docs.text:
Open Menu → Manage Machines → +.

Field Description Name Display name shown in RemoteX (e.g. "Plex Server") Host / IP IP address or hostname of the server SSH User SSH username Port SSH port (default: 22) SSH Key Path Path to your private key file Icon Visual icon to identify this machine

docs.title: SSH key setup
docs.text:
If you don't have an SSH key pair yet, RemoteX can generate one and copy it to the server:

  1. Click Generate SSH key in the machine dialog
  2. Click Copy key to server — enter your password once (it is not stored)
  3. Future connections use the key automatically

docs.title: Testing the connection
docs.text: Click Test in the machine dialog. RemoteX runs echo remotex-ok on the remote host and reports success or the error message.

docs.title: Assigning machines to a button
docs.text: In the button editor, the Target machines section lists all your configured machines plus a Local option. Enable as many targets as you need.

docs.title: Single machine
docs.text: Enable one machine → the button runs the command on that machine directly, no extra click needed.

docs.title: Multiple machines
docs.text: Enable two or more targets → a picker dialog appears each time you click the button, letting you choose which machine(s) to run on.

docs.title: Local + remote
docs.text: Enable Local alongside a machine → the picker includes your local computer as an option. Useful for scripts that work on both.

docs.title: Output modes with SSH
docs.text:
All three output modes work over SSH:

Mode Behaviour Silent Result shown as a toast notification Show output Remote stdout/stderr shown in a dialog after the command finishes Open in terminal RemoteX generates an ssh -t command and opens it in your terminal emulator — full interactive session

docs.title: Run as user
docs.text: In terminal mode you can fill in Run as user — RemoteX prepends sudo -u <user> on the remote command. Useful for running commands as a service account (e.g. www-data, postgres).

docs.title: What data we collect
docs.text: RemoteX collects minimal data, strictly necessary for license validation.

docs.title: At purchase (via LemonSqueezy)
docs.text: Your email address and payment information are collected by LemonSqueezy, our payment processor. We receive only your email address for license validation purposes. We do not receive or store your payment card details.

docs.title: At license activation
docs.text:
When you activate a Pro license, the following data is transmitted to LemonSqueezy's API:

  • Your license key
  • An anonymous device identifier (/etc/machine-id, hashed — not linked to any personal identity)
  • The name of the activation (your hostname, optional)

This data is used solely to verify that your license is valid and to manage your activation slots (max 3 devices).

docs.title: Stored locally on your machine
docs.text:
RemoteX stores the following files in ~/.config/remotex/:

  • buttons.toml — your button configuration
  • machines.toml — your SSH machine definitions (no private keys)
  • profiles.toml — your execution profiles (sudo passwords are encoded with a machine-specific key, not transmitted anywhere)

None of these files are transmitted to any server. The license key and activation metadata are stored locally and used only during validation calls to LemonSqueezy.

docs.title: What we do not collect
docs.text:
• We do not collect analytics or usage data
  • We do not track which commands you run
  • We do not have access to your SSH credentials or private keys
  • We do not use cookies or web tracking

docs.title: Third-party services
docs.text: License validation is handled by LemonSqueezy. Their privacy policy applies to data they process. Validation calls are made at activation and periodically (daily) when the app is running.

docs.title: Data retention
docs.text: We do not store any personal data on our servers. All your configuration data remains on your local machine.

docs.title: Your rights
docs.text: You may request deletion of your account and associated data by contacting us. Deactivating your license removes the device activation record from LemonSqueezy's servers.

docs.title: Activating your license
docs.text:
Welcome to RemoteX Pro! This guide walks you through activating your license for the first time.

!!! tip "What you'll need" - Your license key (sent in your purchase email from LemonSqueezy) - The email address you used when buying - An internet connection (required for the first activation only)

docs.title: Step-by-step activation
docs.text:
1. Open RemoteX — make sure you're running the Pro build, not the Free build. (What's the difference?)

  2. Open Preferences with Ctrl + , or via the hamburger menu → Preferences.

  3. Scroll to the License section.

  4. Paste your license key in the License key field.

  5. Enter the email you used at purchase in the Email field.

    !!! warning "Email must match exactly" We compare what you type against the email LemonSqueezy has on file for the purchase. If it doesn't match, activation is refused — no slot is consumed.

  6. Click Activate Pro.

  7. Within a few seconds, the dialog refreshes and shows:

    • Your license type (Yearly)
    • The expiry date
    • Your activation count (e.g. 1 / 3)

That's it — every Pro feature is now unlocked. SSH machines, multi-machine buttons, themes, backup, MCP server, all available.

docs.title: What happens next
docs.text:
When What happens Right after activation All Pro features unlock immediately Every 24 hours RemoteX silently re-validates your license online (no UI, no slowdown) 30 days before expiry A toast warns you that your subscription is about to renew Day of expiry LemonSqueezy renews your subscription automatically — nothing to do If renewal fails A 3-day grace period kicks in before Pro features lock

If your subscription lapses, your data is never deleted. Buttons, machines, settings — all preserved. Re-activate any time and everything comes back.

docs.title: "This license key is registered to a different email."
docs.text: The email you typed doesn't match the one on the LemonSqueezy purchase. Double-check the receipt email you got from LemonSqueezy and use that exact address (case doesn't matter, but typos do).

docs.title: "You have reached the maximum of 3 activations."
docs.text:
You've used all 3 device slots for this license. Open RemoteX on one of your active devices, go to Preferences → License → Deactivate, then come back and activate here.

If you can't access any of your previously activated devices (lost laptop, OS reinstalled without deactivating, etc.), contact support — we'll free the orphaned slot from the dashboard.

See the full License & Devices guide for every scenario.

docs.title: "Network error — could not reach the license server."
docs.text:
The first activation requires an internet connection (we check the key against LemonSqueezy). Make sure RemoteX can reach api.lemonsqueezy.com — corporate proxies and strict firewalls may block it.

After the first activation, RemoteX works offline. We only re-check once every 24 hours, and there's a 3-day grace period if the network is down.

docs.title: I don't see a "License" section in Preferences
docs.text: You're running the Free build. The Free build has no license system — there's nothing to activate. To use Pro, download the Pro build and run it instead. Your buttons, machines and settings will be picked up automatically (same config directory).
